text: In this video we present a patient with a large incisional hernia after an emergency laparotomy. He will undergo hernia repair with laparoscopic component separation.
The surgeon marks the hernia and important surgical landmarks on the patient's abdomen, preparing for the laparoscopic hernia repair.

The surgeon dissects through the external oblique muscle to reach the internal oblique muscle, developing an avascular intermuscular plane. Air seal trocars are placed, and the external oblique aponeurosis is transected, resulting in the medialization of the rectus abdominis muscles.
The surgeon digitally develops the intermuscular plane over the ribs, preparing for muscular transection. A dilating balloon trocar is placed to further expand the plane.

The surgeon incises and transects the aponeurosis laterally to the semilunar line, avoiding a full thickness abdominal wall defect. The incision extends downward to the inguinal ligament, with additional transection of Scarpa's fascia for maximum mobilization.
The benefits of air seal are quite clear. The pneumo is stable and smoke extraction is without delay.

The surgeon places a five-millimeter port just cranial to the inguinal ligament after sufficiently developing the intermuscular plane. The cranial part of the mobilization involves transecting through the external oblique muscle using a ligature device.
The surgeon further develops the precostal plane before proceeding with the transection of the muscle, ensuring clear separation of tissue layers.
The video briefly shows the right-sided component separation, mirroring the previously demonstrated steps on the left side.
After completing the component separation, the surgeon achieves pneumoperitoneum and performs adhesiolysis to free the abdomen of adhesions. The hernia is then approximated using mattress sutures.
The surgeon places a 20x30 cm Symbiotex mesh intra-abdominally to augment the hernia repair. The mesh is secured to the ventral abdominal wall using Absorbatex fasteners.
